real_insight: When staffing long-horizon work, the qualifier is trained equanimity (decoupling stability from sensor-input polarity), not personality calmness; verify that the operator's equanimity has not arrived at the cost of care.
could_act_on_tomorrow: Yes — the next time we are evaluating tech-lead candidates for a multi-year migration, explicitly test for the polarity-decoupling capacity (how does the candidate respond to dashboard movement?) and equally explicitly test for care (does the candidate engage with users, with teammates, with the system itself?). The verse separates these two checks.
is_new_or_sharply_named: The 'equanimity-with-care, not flatness' framing is sharper than the usual 'find calm tech leads' content. The verse explicitly distinguishes trained equanimity from cold detachment — and the falsifiability section calls out the failure mode of selecting on calm-without-care, which is a real hiring failure I have seen.
objection: none. The counter-example (short-horizon work where reactive operators may be preferable) is correctly carved out — the verse does not claim equanimity is always superior; it claims it is the qualifier for a specific kind of work.
verdict_reason: Specific phenomenon (long-horizon work needs trained equanimity), specific corrective (select on it explicitly + verify care has not been sacrificed), specific test (does the candidate's equanimity coexist with their care for users/teammates?). Senior engineer would adopt this in hiring.
